items.title: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists
items.text:
A hands-on workshop at the first Imageomics Conference, hosted by the Imageomics Institute.

  • Dates: April 16 (1:00–5:00 PM) & April 17 (8:00 AM–1:00 PM), 2026
  • Location: Energy Advancement and Innovation Center, The Ohio State University, Columbus, OH
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: About
items.text: The Imageomics Institute has built a powerful suite of open-source tools for image-based biological research. This workshop is dedicated to bridging the gap between these computational tools and biological discovery. By partnering directly with biologists across all career stages, we aim to pressure-test existing workflows, uncover novel applications, and ensure these tools are as intuitive as they are innovative.
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: Tutorials
items.text: Each tutorial walks through a specific Imageomics tool, from setup to hands-on use with example data.
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: Classification
items.text: Tutorial Description pybioclip Classify focal species and generate image embeddings programmatically using BioCLIP models without complex ML infrastructure.
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: Image Embedding Exploration
items.text: Tutorial Description Image Embedding Explorer Explore image embeddings interactively—try different models, projections, and see which images cluster together or apart.
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: Segmentation & Known Trait Extraction
items.text: Tutorial Description SST Segment traits from specimen images and apply automatic landmarking.
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: Interpretability
items.text: Tutorial Description Prompt-CAM Explore fine-grained trait distinctions between different specified species. Finer-CAM Better understand discriminative traits between similar species.
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: Similarity Search & Discovery
items.text: Tutorial Description BioCLIP Search Lite Given a query image, find the most visually similar organisms across 200M+ samples from the TreeofLife dataset. SAE Discover what traits are common among organisms across a collection of images.
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: Workshop Structure
items.text:
• Day 1 (Thursday, April 16): Live demonstrations and guided tutorials. Open to all Imageomics Conference participants.
  • Day 2 (Friday, April 17): Interactive, hands-on deep dive with a smaller group. Participants work with their own data and use cases.
path: Designing for Discovery: Shaping Imageomics Tools for Biologists

items.title: Finer-CAM
items.text: Better explanation for classification decisions on similar species.

items.title: Learning Objectives
items.text:
By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to:

  1. Understand how Finer-CAM extracts the parts of an organism image that are most useful for telling one similar species from another.
  2. Use the provided tutorial or demo to create visual explanation maps for a species image.
  3. Judge whether the highlighted regions match biologically meaningful traits provided by ecologists or field experts.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• We provide demos and a tutorial notebook to support hands-on use, so there will be no need for setting up local environments.
  • If local environments are preferred, you can install the package by pip install grad-cam.

items.title: Setup
items.text:
• Prepare organismal images of interest with corresponding species labels and upload them to Google Drive, so that they can be used in Colab to train models and produce explanation. A training-test split of images would be preferred.
  • If you want to run the method locally, you can train a classifier with the prepared images. We use a linear classifier connected to a DINOv2 backbone in our example.
  • You can also use the datasets and models prepared by us, following the instructions at Finer-CAM repo.

items.title: Background
items.text: Finer-CAM is designed for explaining model decisions in situations where the species of interest look very similar. Standard explanation tools tend to highlight generic organism regions instead of the subtle trait that truly separates similar species. Finer-CAM identifies discriminative traits by asking directly, what makes the species different from a similar one.

items.title: Steps
items.text:
1. Train a neural network with prepared images.
  2. Select an image of the organism you want to inspect. Choose a case where the distinction is subtle, such as two closely related species.
  3. Run Finer-CAM and Grad-CAM through the provided notebook to generate visual explanation maps.
  4. Inspect the highlighted regions and ask whether they align with known field traits, such as wing color, markings, shape, or other subtle morphology. Compare the regions generated by Finer-CAM and Grad-CAM and check whether the results of Finer-CAM reflect more discriminative traits.
  5. Use quantitative metrics to evaluate the effectiveness of Finer-CAM.
  6. Additionally, we show how Finer-CAM can be used to extract traits with natural language.

items.title: Summary
items.text: Finer-CAM is a visual explanation tool for fine-grained recognition. It helps reveal which visible traits a model is using to separate similar species. The workflow is lightweight and straightforward. Once there is a model, Finer-CAM will be a low-latent tool to interpret and check whether the model's reasoning matches biological intuition.
